secsa__001_a.text:
Background
Unfractionated heparin (UFH) is an intravenous anticoagulant on the World Health Organisation model list of
essential medicines. UFH is prescribed to prevent and treat thrombosis. UFH is a leading cause of
anticoagulation-related adverse drug events in the UK and health systems around the world. In England and
Wales, over the period 1/1/2014-15/11/2022, 603 adverse incidents attributed to UFH were reported to the
National Reporting and Learning System. The risks associated with UFH are due to a fine balance between
efficacy and toxicity. Therefore, UFH infusions require regular (up to 4 hourly) monitoring through a blood test, the
Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (APTT). However, the APTT test is not a reliable test for UFH and was used
historically to inform the dosing schedules for UFH.
There is now (1) a more reliable test, the anti-Xa assay, which better reflects how patients are responding to UFH
treatment, and (2) there are better methods for working out UFH drug dosing, using Population Pharmacokinetic
Modelling. There is therefore an urgent need to update the way UFH is dosed and monitored in clinical practice, so
patient care is improved.
Aim
To develop an

secsa__001_b.text:
IV UFH infusion dosing and monitoring protocol based on the Population Pharmacokinetic
Modelling that is implemented in hospitals.
Methods
I will conduct a three-study programme of research. In study 1, using data from hospital records from patients
administered an UFH infusion, I will develop a population pharmacokinetic model for UFH. Following multi-centre
model validation, I will conduct simulations from the final model to determine the optimal dosing strategy to
achieve the desired UFH therapeutic range.
In study 2, I will conduct a retrospective study of all UFH incidents reported to the National Reporting and Learning
System over a 10-year period to identify the number and nature of the adverse events relating to UFH, including
system failures and defences breached in each incident. This analysis will identify common problems and themes
associated with the use of UFH use. The analysis and the lessons from these events will help inform the
development of a contemporary UFH protocol.
In study 3, using the findings from studies 1 and 2, with input from patients, members of the public, carers, doctors,
nurses, pharmacist’s, laboratory staff and porters, I will co-develop a conte

secsa__001_c.text:
mporary UFH protocol. I will then
conduct an assessment of acceptability and feasibility during implementation into routine clinical practice guided
by the Consolidated Framework for Implementation Research.
Impact/Dissemination
My research will lead to a step-change in how UFH is dosed and monitored, leading to effective and safer care. My
work has translatability beyond the UK. I anticipate the protocol will become the standard dosing and monitoring
protocol for all UFH infusions. Findings will be disseminated to clinicians/patients/public and policy makers,
ensuring immediate clinical relevance and translation for patient benefit. Dissemination will be through the website
I establish, through social media and organisations such as the British Society of Haematology, Thrombosis UK,
the Anticoagulation Forum and the International Society of Thrombosis and Haemostasis.

secdrp__001_a.text:
1. What is the problem being addressed?
Unfractionated heparin (UFH) was the first anticoagulant to become available in clinical practice for the
management of thrombosis. One in 4 people die from conditions caused by thrombosis. UFH is included on the
WHO list of essential medications as one of the most critical and effective medications necessary to meet the
needs of a healthcare system.1 UFH requires administration as a continuous intravenous infusion and remains the
anticoagulant agent of choice in select, often high-risk patients, i.e. those in critical care, patients who undergo
cardiac surgery, and those in renal failure.
However, UFH is a leading cause of anticoagulation related adverse drug events in the UK and health systems
around the world. UFH is classed as one of 6 drugs most frequently involved in serious medication events2, 3 and is
classified by the Institute for Safe Medication Practices as a “high-alert” medication.4 Of all records submitted to
the American National Error database 2.1% were attributed to UFH, with 5.5% of these incidents causing patient
harm.5 In the National Reporting and Learning System database for England and Wales, from 1/1/2014 to
15/11/202

secdrp__001_b.text:
2, 603 adverse incidents including deaths were attributed to UFH.
Despite guidelines, adverse incidents attributed to UFH remain common.6 Indeed, in my hospital, there were three
deaths as a direct result of issues with intravenous UFH within a 6-month period in 2019-2020. Dosing and
monitoring are frequently cited as significant factors contributing to UFH adverse events. This stems in part from
the fine line that exists between UFH efficacy and toxicity.
To avoid adverse events and ensure therapeutic dosing, UFH requires regular monitoring via blood testing.
Additionally, an intravenous UFH infusion involves a number of tasks for multiple members of the clinical team,
which makes administration complex and is associated with increased risk and workload. A failure mode and
study7
effects analysis has mapped and identified 233 potential failures and 737 unique potential causes,
associated with the use of an intravenous UFH infusion, 45 of which were classified as significant.
Importantly, the original dosing schedules and the monitoring blood test for UFH dosing are now both out of date
yet still in use in clinical practice. There is now a blood test, the anti-Xa assay, which bette

secdrp__001_c.text:
r reflects how patients
are responding to UFH treatment. There are also better methods for working out drug dosing, known as Population
Pharmacokinetic Modelling (PPM). As a result, the current UFH dosing schedule is in need of revision to improve
dosing accuracy and monitoring and thus patient safety and outcomes.
2. Why is this research important?
In England, medication errors are estimated to cost £98.5 million, account for 181,626 bed-days, and contribute to
1708 hospital deaths every year.8 Medication-related errors are thought to cost an estimated 42 billion U.S. dollars
worldwide every year.3
In 2017, the WHO launched the Global Patient Safety Challenge: Medication Without Harm,9 This initiative focuses
on 4 medication safety domains: (i) patients and the public, (ii) medicines, (iii) health care professionals and (iv)
systems and practices of medication management, with 3 priority areas: “polypharmacy,” “high-risk situations,” and
“transitions of care.” UFH is a significant contributor to medication adverse-events and falls under domains (ii), (iii)
and (iv) and the high-risk situation priority.
Through the development and implementation of a contemporary UFH dosing protoco

secdrp__001_d.text:
l, I anticipate the number of
UFH incidents will be minimised, patient safety will be improved, staff workload will be reduced resulting in
improved patient outcomes and care efficiencies.
Preparatory PPI work for my fellowship proposal showed that patients and members of the public have positive
attitudes about engaging in medication safety. When seeking guidance about my fellowship topic, patients stated
that this is an important area for research including optimising implementation into practice, so there is translation
of findings for patient benefit. Patients and members of the public also recommended that they are actively
involved in the development of a new UFH protocol.
3. Review of existing evidence
UFH was introduced into clinical practice >50 years ago and despite the availability of newer anticoagulants,
remains the drug of choice in hospitalised patients who are critically ill, those undergoing cardiac surgery, and
renal patients. This is due to its short half-life and favourable profile in renal failure.10
UFH exhibits a marked variation in anticoagulant response among individuals and therefore requires monitoring to
ensure adequate anticoagulation (efficacy), whilst

secdrp__001_e.text:
minimising the risk of bleeding (toxicity).11-13 This is achieved
using a biomarker as a proxy for plasma concentration. Historically, this biomarker has been the activated partial
thromboplastin time (APTT), however APTT is now widely recognised as a sub-optimal biomarker for UFH.14, 15 In
recent years, the anti-Xa assay, calibrated for UFH is replacing the APTT as the monitoring test for UFH.
Research shows that the time to reach the therapeutic range and number of subsequent dose changes required
are less with anti-Xa compared to APTT monitoring.16-19 A retrospective cohort study of nearly 20 000 patients
concluded that cases monitored by anti-Xa were less likely to require a blood transfusion than patients monitored
by APTT after controlling for age, sex and invasive procedures.20
A weight-based UFH dosing schedule is widely recommended and used in clinical practice.21 However, this dosing
schedule was developed when modern pharmacokinetic techniques were not available and used the historical
biomarker test (APTT).14, 15 Research shows that when this weight-based dosing schedule is followed only 50% of
patients are in the therapeutic range within 24 hours.22, 23 Other factors,

secdrp__001_f.text:
such as age and sex could have important
implications for the dosing of UFH.24-26
Population Pharmacokinetic Modelling (PPM) provides an opportunity to address this dosing problem. PPM is the
science of developing and applying mathematical and statistical models to characterise, understand and predict a
drug’s pharmacokinetic, pharmacodynamic and biomarker outcome behaviour.27, 28 Following data collection from
the target population, the PPM method develops a pharmacokinetic model to simulate the optimal dose or dosing
regimen of the drug in question in the target population. The PPM methodology is endorsed by the European
Medicines Agency and the U.S Food and Drug Administration during the drug development process and is
additionally applied by clinicians, to address important drug dosing issues at the clinical coalface.29, 30 To date 3
PPM studies have been published focused on UFH,31-33 none of which are based on the anti-Xa assay, making my
proposal novel.
Despite advances in anticoagulation therapy in the last 10 years, UFH remains the drug of choice in hospitalised
patients in select, high-risk hospitalised patient groups. Indeed its use saw a resurgence during the COVID
pan

secdrp__001_g.text:
demic.34 However UFH remains a medication associated with patient harm and its dosing and monitoring
underpin this. Using the anti-Xa assay and modern pharmacokinetic methods, it is important to revisit the dosing
and monitoring approach taken with UFH, in order to improve patient safety and outcomes.
4. Aim and objectives
Aim
To develop a contemporary intravenous UFH infusion dosing and monitoring protocol based on anti-Xa monitoring
and population pharmacokinetic modelling and evaluate its implementation into clinical practice.
Objectives
 To characterise the pharmacokinetics of intravenous UFH using PPM
 To analyse commonly reported problems associated with the use of intravenous UFH using the National
Reporting and Learning System to inform the UFH protocol
 To co-devlope (with patients and clinicians) and to implement a contemporary UFH dosing and monitoring
protocol across King's College Hospital, Guy's and St.Thomas' Hospitals and Royal Brompton and
Harefield Hospitals.
5. Research plan
Study 1 – Population pharmacokinetics of UFH in adults
Research question: What are the population pharmacokinetics of UFH in adults?
I will conduct a multi-centre PPM study of UFH.
Study s

secdrp__001_h.text:
etting
I will collect data on patients commenced on an UFH infusion at King’s College Hospital (KCH), Guy’s and St.
Thomas’ Hospitals (GSTT), Royal Brompton and Harefield Hospitals (RBHH) – which comprise 6 hospital sites in
total. These organisations serve diverse patient populations across London and the Southeast. Collectively, these
organisations house a major trauma centre, a regional kidney disease centre and the largest UK specialist heart
and lung centre.
Patient population
I will compile two datasets, a model building dataset (KCH) and a model validation dataset (GSTT & RBHH).
Inclusion criteria
For model development, I will collect retrospective data on all adult patients commenced on an UFH infusion at
KCH with anti-Xa monitoring. Patients will be identified through laboratory data of anti-Xa (UFH) assay requests,
over the time period 13/10/2021 – 12/10/2024.
From 1/11/2024 to 31/07/2025, I will prospectively identify all new patients commencing UFH at GSTT and RBHH
and collect their UFH infusion data. This data will comprise the model validation dataset.
I will exclude patients <18 years, and those prescribed UFH during extra-corporeal membrane oxygenation from
both dat

secdrp__001_i.text:
asets.
Data Collection
I will collect data on the time course of the UFH infusion along with associated anti-Xa concentrations, dosage and
subsequent dose changes, until the conclusion of the infusion. I will use the anti-Xa UFH concentration as a
surrogate marker for profiling and modelling UFH’s pharmacokinetic profile. I have already tested the feasibility of
my data collection methods through the completion of two safety audits, at KCH in 2022 and 2023.
I will collect the following variables: age, gender, ethnicity, indication for UFH, weight, height, serum creatinine,
potassium, urea, albumin, bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase, haemoglobin, platelet count and coagulation screen at
UFH infusion initiation.
Sample size and analysis
To date, three PPM studies have been published on UFH,31-33 with none using the anti-Xa assay. The sample
sizes of these previous PPM studies are: 50 (using activated clotting time), 64 (using APTT) and 41 (using a
factor-IIa assay). The two safety audits I conducted at KCH, identified 290 patients over a 1 year period who would
be eligible for the present study.
Considering the timeframe outlined of my fellowship proposal, I anticipate at least 870 pat

secdrp__001_j.text:
ients contributing data
into the model-building dataset. Assuming each patient contributes 3 anti-Xa readings (minimum) there will be
2610 anti-Xa data points for PPM modelling purposes. This significantly exceeds any previous work in this field.
For the purposes of model validation, I anticipate prospective data on at least 435 patients over the 9 month period
from GSTT and RBHH.
I will conduct PPM using NONMEM® software programme. This takes the anti-Xa results collected from the
patient population to estimate primary pharmacokinetic parameters.
Specifically, I will explore several structural base models, and will select the model which best fits the data.
Goodness of fit plots, a statistical improvement in the fit of the model to the data using the objective function
(minus twice the log-likelihood of the data), assessment of the precision of the parameter estimates and residual
variability will be the criterion used to evaluate and choose the base model to take forward for full covariate
analysis.
Next, I will conduct a covariate analysis, choosing covariates which have a mechanistic meaning, e.g. age,
ethnicity, weight. This analysis will involve a classical graphical approach

secdrp__001_k.text:
, plotting individual estimates of the
pharmacokinetic parameter’s random effects against each of the covariates being considered for model inclusion.
Each selected covariate will be tested by univariate addition into the base model to confirm relevance. A decrease
in objective function value of at least 6.64 (p<0.01), will be required to retain the covariate in the intermediate
model.
All significant covariates will then simultaneously be added to the base model and their continued relevance will be
evaluated using a stepwise backward elimination method, where each covariate will be removed singularly. An
increase in the objective function value of greater than 10.82 (p<0.001) will be required to retain the covariate in
the final model.
Both the base and the final models will be evaluated using a nonparametric bootstrap (1000 replicates). The final
model will additionally be evaluated using a visual predictive check, where the 5th, 50th and 95th prediction
intervals, simulated from the posterior distribution of the final model parameter estimates will be overlaid with the
5th, 50th and 95th percentiles from the observed data. A well performing model would see the observed
percenti

secdrp__001_l.text:
les and simulated prediction intervals superimposed.
Model validation
I will then use the prospective model validation data set (GSTT and RBHH) to evaluate the predictive performance
of the model developed and thus its effectiveness. No additional fitting will be done. The diagnostic plots and a
visual predictive check will be generated as described in the model development section. Model-predicted target
concentrations will be computed using the developed model as a prior and information from the validation dataset
only. These predictions will be compared with the observed concentrations by calculating the prediction error, the
mean precision error and the root mean square error.
Finally, simulations from the final UFH PK model developed will be conducted to determine the optimal dosing
strategy which get patients into the desired therapeutic range.
Study 2 - Incidence and analysis of UFH adverse events reported to the National Reporting and Learning
System
Research question: What are the number and nature of adverse events associated with the use of intravenous
UFH, which can inform the development and implementation of a contemporary UFH dosing and monitoring
protocol into clini

secdrp__001_m.text:
cal practice?
I will conduct a retrospective study of all UFH incidents reported to the National Reporting and Learning System
(NRLS).
Data source
The NRLS is a unique resource of patient safety incidents from England and Wales, allowing scrutiny of incidents
that have occurred in the National Health Service, so improvements are made and fed back into clinical practice.
All NHS organisations and staff are encouraged to report to the NRLS any occurrence of harm or potential harm to
a patient as a ‘patient safety incident’, defined as ‘any unintended or unexpected incident which could have, or did,
lead to harm for one or more patients receiving NHS care’ since 2002. Each NRLS incident report includes
structured information: demographic and administrative; the circumstances of occurrence; a categorisation of
causation; an assessment of the degree of actual harm; and action taken, or planned, to investigate or prevent a
recurrence. Additionally, a section of the report allows the reporting staff member to state in free text, what
happened and why they think it happened. Data are anonymised to remove details that identify any patients or
staff.
I will request a report from NHS England

secdrp__001_n.text:
on all incidents, during the period 1/1/2014-31/12/2023 which include any
of the following terms: unfractionated heparin; heparin; heparin AND unfractionated; heparin AND sodium; heparin
AND calcium. As part of the request, the following fields will be requested in the output: Incident ID, Care setting,
Date, Location, Incident category, Description of what happened, Actions preventing reoccurrence, Apparent
causes, Speciality, Reported degree of harm, Source of notification, Medication process, Medication error
category, Approved name, Proprietary name, Year of occurrence, Month of occurrence, Staff type, Staff status,
Reporting year.
Feasibility: over the period 1/1/2014-15/11/2022, 603 adverse incidents were identified in the NRLS database,
when searched using the term ‘unfractionated heparin’ (personal communication with NHS England). NHS
England have confirmed that they will provide the report for the purposes of my fellowship.
Inclusion criteria
All reports outputted over the period outlined, with the search terms described, relating to the use of an
intravenous UFH.
Analysis
The setting (adult/paediatric), ward type (e.g. ICU, general medicine), degree of harm reported, and

secdrp__001_o.text:
the year in
which the incident occurred will be described.
Furthermore, the principal stage of the medication use process in which the patient safety incident occurred, will
be categorised according to the following: prescribing, preparation, administration, monitoring, dose adjustment,
documentation.35 Each incident will additionally be categorised according to the NCC MERP index for categorising
errors.36
medication These will be done in addition to the pre-specified NRLS categorisation, to assess whether the
detail provided by the NRLS reports allows a more granular categorisation of the events recorded.
Finally, where sufficient detail has been provided, the root cause of each incident will be analysed using the
‘Fishbone’ methodology,37 aiming to identify where possible, the specific system failures in each case.
The findings and lessons from this analysis will feed into the development and implementation of the contemporary
UFH protocol.
Study 3 – Co-develop, implement and evaluate the contemporary UFH protocol
Aim: To co-develop, implement and evaluate the contemporary UFH protocol at KCH, GSTT and RBHH.
Rationale and underpinning theory
The theory guiding the implementation

secdrp__001_p.text:
and evaluation components of my fellowship is the Consolidated
Framework for Implementation Research (CFIR).38 As a determinant framework, CFIR provides an appropriate
means to predict and/or explain barriers/facilitators to implementation effectiveness. The CFIR constructs reflect
factors most likely to influence intervention implementation, with a particular focus on context, and therefore
uptake. The CFIR is flexible, and the framework can be tailored to the specific intervention design, factors, and
context being studied.38
A logic model for study 3 is outlined in figure 1.
Preparatory work
Initially, I will apply the CFIR framework to each hospital site, to map the internal and external factors that might
influence implementation of the UFH protocol and to inform the data collected.
This will identify key stakeholders at each site driving the use of UFH, who I will then invite to form a heparin
stakeholder group across the study sites.
The stakeholder group will be multidisciplinary: patients, carers, doctors, nurses, pharmacists, porters (involved in
sample transportation) and laboratory staff.
This stakeholder group will meet 12 times over years 2 and 3 of my fellowship and

secdrp__001_q.text:
will co-develop and implement
the UFH at the study sites.
Study 3 will be completed in 3 phases.
Phase I - understanding needs and context
To contextualise the use of UFH at the participating hospitals, I will address the following objectives:
1. To compare and contrast the UFH protocols currently in place at each hospital site
2. To observe how UFH infusions are currently used in practice, including monitoring practices and understand
the perceived problems with UFH infusions experienced by front-line staff
3. To explore the nature of UFH incidents reported at the participating hospitals
Objective 1
I will obtain current UFH protocols in operation at each hospital site and analyse their content. Components will be
categorised into (1)indications for which UFH is suggested, (2)recommended UFH dosing, (3)how the UFH
infusion is set-up and administered, (4)target anti-Xa concentrations, (5)suggested changes in the dose rate
according to results, (6)frequency of monitoring, (7)how samples are processed and reported by the laboratory
and (8)where staff can access expert help.
This content will be entered into a data collection tool in Microsoft Excel and analysed for similarities and

secdrp__001_r.text:
differences between hospitals.
Output: A descriptive map39 of what commonality/differences exist in UFH protocols across the sites.
Objective 2
I will conduct a multi-site qualitative go-long interview study,40 which will focus on how clinical staff deliver UFH
infusions in practice and what barriers/enablers there would be to a new UFH protocol.
Participants: I will purposively sample front-line clinical staff who are involved in the delivery of the UFH infusions.
Data collection will combine observations of UFH delivery in practice with conversations, making it possible to
observe spatial practices in-situ while gathering insights from the interviewee. I will conduct 24 go-along interviews
(3 nurses, 2 doctors, 1 pharmacist, 1 lab staff, and 1 porter from each hospital).
Incentives: £25 e-gift card provided to each volunteer.
Data collection: I will develop a topic guide, in consultation with the stakeholder group and guided by CFIR.
Data analysis: I will follow principles of deductive reasoning.41 This will involve an iterative dialogue whereby
preliminary lines of data interpretation are tested with reference to existing theory to confirm or to extent
explanatory reasoning. I w

secdrp__001_s.text:
ill relate back codes and themes identified to the domains of CFIR.
Output: An understanding of what issues are faced by the front-line staff in delivering UFH infusions safely and
how receptive they are to a new UFH protocol.
Objective 3
To build a picture of the specific nature of UFH adverse events occurring at each hospital, I will interrogate the
error reporting system at each site to identify all incidents linked to UFH at each hospital over the period 01/2022 –
12/2024.
The data analysis for this will follow that previously described in study 2.
Output: This will complement the findings from study 2 of the NRLS analysis and provide an insight into any
particular problems associated with UFH at each hospital.
Following completion of phase I, coupled with the findings from studies 1 and 2, a contemporary UFH infusion
protocol will be co-developed by the stakeholder group and ratified at KCH. The protocol will be launched at KCH,
through a planned implementation.
Phase II – pilot and assess
Following implementation at one of my study sites, I will evaluate acceptability, fidelity, safety and appropriateness
3 months into implementation.
Acceptability
I will conduct a cross-sect

secdrp__001_t.text:
ional study asking staff involved in the delivery of UFH protocols to complete the
Theoretical Framework of Acceptability questionnaire.42
Sampling: I will purposively sample 50 members of staff (nurses, doctors, pharmacists, porters, and laboratory
staff) at the study site in cardiology, ICU and renal specialities.
Incentives: £15 e-gift card for completion of questionnaire
Analysis: The Theoretical Framework of Acceptability questionnaire comprises 7 constructs: affective attitude,
burden, ethicality, perceived effectiveness, intervention coherence, self-efficacy and opportunity costs. There is
also a single question around general acceptability. The scales are on a 5-point Likert scale, with the negative
extreme descriptor scored at 1. A single acceptability score will be computed generating a mean score of the 7
constructs. Items assessing burden and opportunity costs will be reverse scored (higher score indicates higher
acceptability). I will correlate this score with the overall acceptability item. I will use descriptive statistics to
represent the findings.
Output: An indication of how acceptable the contemporary UFH protocol is to front-line staff at the pilot site.
Fidelit

secdrp__001_u.text:
y
Underpinned by the Conceptual Framework for Implementation Fidelity,43 a fidelity checklist will be compiled and
agreed by the UFH stakeholder group. This checklist will primarily assess whether staff adhere to the (core and
non-core) aspects of the protocol, as defined by the stakeholder group.
Data collection: I will assess fidelity by retrospectively reviewing all UFH infusions administered between weeks 4
and 11 at KCH during the pilot period, by reviewing the electronic medical / prescribing / laboratory records using
the compiled checklist.
Analysis: The checklist will have components relating to the prescribing, preparation, administration, monitoring,
reporting of results and adjustments of the UFH infusion. Scoring of the fidelity checklist will be computed by
selections of ‘Yes’, ‘No’ and ‘Nondelivered’. A total score will be generated based on the number of delivered
components divided by the number of components that were deliverable and multiplied by 100 to give a % fidelity
score.
Output: An assessment of the degree to which the UFH protocol is delivered as intended.
Safety and Appropriateness
I will evaluate appropriateness of the contemporary UFH protocol through

secdrp__001_v.text:
prescribing data and safety through any
incident reports which emerge during the implementation.
Data collection: I will identify all UFH infusion prescriptions through the e-prescribing system at KCH,
retrospectively and I will assess:
-% of patients reaching target anti-Xa concentrations within 6 and 24 hours of commencing the infusion when the
dosing protocol is followed
-% of patients overshooting (>1 IU/mL) or undershooting (<0.1 IU/mL) when the dosing protocol is followed
Through the incident report systems in the hospitals, the number and nature of any UFH adverse incidents
reported will be collated and investigated.
Analysis: Descriptive statistics will be used to report findings. The number and nature of any safety incidents will
be described.
Output: An indication of how well the dosing protocol is performing when followed.
The findings from the acceptability, fidelity, safety and appropriateness studies will be triangulated and discussed
with the stakeholder group to inform what, if any, refinements should be made to the UFH protocol, before ratifying
and launching at GSTT and RBHH.
Phase III – full implementation
The acceptability, fidelity, safety and appropriateness a

secdrp__001_w.text:
ssessments, as outlined in phase 2, will be completed
across the 3 hospitals - 3 months post implementation for GSTT and RBHH and 6 months for KCH.
As well as generating data for each specific hospital, differences in the findings between hospitals and sites will be
compared.
Following the conclusion of my fellowship, fidelity and appropriateness will be re-assessed at each hospital, 1 year
following implementation. These assessments will follow the methodology as described previously and conducted
by either my undergraduate or masters students at KCL, following appropriate training.
A key aim of my fellowship is to have a standardised UFH protocol across the health service. Therefore,
penetration of the protocol into the NHS will be studied 3 years following the implementation of the protocol at
KCH, GSTT and RBHH. My research students will conduct this, along with fidelity, safety, appropriateness and
acceptability studies, under my supervision.
6. Dissemination, Outputs and anticipated Impact
Outputs
My fellowship will lead to the development and implementation of a contemporary UFH dosing protocol. There will
be significant publications from my fellowship focused on: (i) PPM wo

secdrp__001_x.text:
rk of UFH, (ii) analysis and learning from past
UFH adverse events reported to NRLS, and (iii) implementation and evaluation of the UFH protocol into practice.
Dissemination
Early on in my fellowship, I will establish a website, which will provide live information on the progress of my work
to members of the public, patients and healthcare professionals, so there is transparency on how the UFH protocol
has been developed and what steps are being taken to implement the findings.
My PPI group will advise and co-develop the patient and members of the public material for the public areas of the
website, as well as advise and co-develop the materials disseminated through the Thrombosis UK website,
Facebook and X accounts.
The website will have a clinician specific section, which will update healthcare professionals on the programme of
work and what results are emerging. The transparency of this will lead to greater penetration of the final UFH
protocol into clinical practice, once implemented.
Throughout my fellowship and beyond, the findings will be disseminated as oral presentations and posters in real-
time at appropriate conferences, e.g. ISTH, so that the results undergo wide peer

secdrp__001_y.text:
review and be scrutinised. The
findings will also be published in peer review journals.
I am a member of the BSH guideline writing group for Measurement of Heparin and other non-Coumarin
anticoagulants and their effects on tests of Haemostasis. I am therefore well placed to ensure the work from my
fellowship informs the BSH update of the UFH guideline.
Impact
Through completing the work outlined in my fellowship, patients will receive safer and more effective care. The
UFH dosing protocol developed will be available for centres to use and through the due diligence applied in its
development, will be universally applicable across the health service in the UK and around the world.
7. Project management
I will be responsible for project management and completion of the work proposed. With the support of my
research support mentors, we will meet every 4-6 weeks as a group, to discuss progress and I will take on-board
advice and guidance provided. I have extensive experience of seeing research studies to completion and I am
confident that this will be the case here.
Additionally, during year 2 of my fellowship I will form a heparin stakeholder group formed from the multiple
centres taki

secdrp__001_z.text:
ng part, who will meet regularly during year 2 and 3 and co-develop and implement the UFH protocol in
their respective organisations.
My PPI panel will additionally meet every 6 months to specifically discuss work plans relating to PPI in my
fellowship which will be chaired by a constitute member of the group. This panel will also be part of the wider
heparin stakeholder group.
8. Ethics
Ethics approval and governance approvals will be sought, prior to commencing studies 1, 2 and 3. In order to
commence work soon into the fellowship, ethics approval for study 1 will be sought prior to the start of the
fellowship.
9. Success criteria
(i) a Population Pharmacokinetic model of UFH in adult patients
(ii) an understanding of number and nature of what has gone wrong in the past with UFH infusions and learning
lessons from these incidents
(iii) co-developing and implementing a contemporary UFH protocol into clinical practice
The risks identified are associated with success criteria (i) and (iii). The retrospective nature of the model
development dataset means that data could be missing. However, this has been mitigated, by already completing
2 safety audits at KCH, which demonstrates that

secdrp__001_{.text:
all the data required for PPM purposes is present and obtainable.
This is the same for GSTT and RBHH as well.
Implementation of anything new is challenging and indeed is an element of the work that is a key development
target for me to learn about, as part of my fellowship. There is a risk that the UFH dosing protocol is not taken-up
into clinical practice. To mitigate this, the CFIR is guiding the implementation and a stakeholder group formed from
the 3 organisations will co-develop and implement the protocol. This will ensure ‘buy-in’ to the final protocol and
will be fully supportive of its implementation and facilitate this into clinical practice. The results from all 3 studies
will be made widely available, so others can independently scrutinise the results and conclusions drawn, which
should ensure there is wide penetration of the final protocol.

secppi__001_a.text:
Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
I convened two PPI events to inform the research outlined in my fellowship. One comprised 3 patients who were
familiar with heparin, including a representative from the charity Thrombosis UK and the second group comprised
3 members of the public.
Following a presentation on the background and what work was being proposed, both groups confirmed that the
proposed research was an important area to tackle and particularly liked the idea of using healthcare data to
address the research questions of studies 1 and 2, with the members of the public group stating it made good
economic sense. Both groups also commented that if anonymised data is used for something like this and helps
the NHS then makes a lot of sense to do. The anticoagulated group confirmed that learning from past mistakes
and analysing information from the National Learning and Reporting System was an important component in the
work proposed.
The PPI groups commented on the fact that research implementation was not something they had thought about
before but said this was crucial and “…makes total sense that you

secppi__001_b.text:
spend time taking the findings and
implementing into clinical practice.” Some concluding remarks from the groups: “Very worthwhile project.” “Why
hasn’t this been done already!?”
Following PPI review, the following changes were made to research proposal:
-making clearer what will be learnt from study 2 and how that can be applied to the final protocol developed
-ensuring that nurses, doctors, pharmacists, porters and laboratory staff are involved the UFH heparin protocol
development. And to actively involve patients and members of the public in the process.
The 2 PPI events additionally confirmed that the public/patient voice in the work is important, and the importance
of continued involvement was emphasised; which has now led to a PPI panel being formed with representation
from Thrombosis UK to continue to input into my fellowship and be PPI stakeholders in the development of the
UFH protocol and as research champions.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users and the public will be actively involved in the
proposed research, including any training and support provided
Following a recommendation from the preparatory PPI work, a panel comprising of patients and mem

secppi__001_c.text:
bers of the
public has been formed. This panel comprises 2 patients who have previously received UFH and 2 members of
the public and will be actively involved in the development of the UFH protocol.
The panel, chaired by a constituent member, will be supported with their PPI activities through meetings held
every 6 months throughout the lifecycle of my fellowship. These meetings will provide an opportunity to get
updates on the work and impending work streams.
The panel will also lead and co-develop the materials for public dissemination, from the start of the proposed work
outlining to patients and the public why the work is being completed, to when results begin to emerge. This will
primarily be led through the public section of the website that will be established, ensuring that the information and
materials that are posted there is communicated in plain English. The panel will also provide advice on how best to
liaise with organisations like Thrombosis UK and will co-develop the material posted on Thrombosis UK
communication channels (Facebook, X and website), so there is wide-spread dissemination through Thrombosis
UK communication channels, as well.
The panel will also attend

secppi__001_d.text:
and contribute to the unfractionated heparin stakeholder meetings and co-develop the
UFH protocol. Finally, the findings from the implementation work will be presented at the Quality and Safety in
Healthcare conference. The chair of the PPI panel will present the findings with me at this conference.
To enable the PPI panel members, following a needs assessment, training will be provided through the Imperial
College London public involvement training aimed at patients and members of the public. The panel will be
recompensed for their time for undertaking this role, as per NIHR guidelines. At the conclusion of my fellowship,
the involvement and impact of PPI involvement will be evaluated through a workshop undertaking a cube
framework exercise, the findings of which will also be published, so any learning can benefit the wider research
community.
If it is considered not appropriate and meaningful to actively involve patients/services users, carers and
the public in your proposed research, please justify why
Not applicable.

sectdars__001_a.text:
Proposed training and development programme
Gap analysis
In order to establish myself as an international research leader, a gap analysis was conducted. Using the vitae
researcher development framework, the following were identified under the domain of personal effectiveness:
i. Has national, international and policy-making network connections with academic and non-academic
bodies and organisations.
ii. Has influential connections with significant bodies and organisations.
In addition, I have identified that I have little knowledge of the field of implementation science and the field of
epidemiology, which I will formally study during my fellowship.
Through completing the work proposed in my fellowship, the step-change in my career will occur, which will
establish me as an international leader and authority on anticoagulation therapy and with that, have exceptional
influence, with input sought from policy makers, as befits an internationally recognised researcher.
Research support
Suitable research support mentors for each stage of the research proposed in the application have been
identified, who would not only enhance the work undertaken but are international research leaders in
sectdars__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_b.text:
their own
fields and prefect role-models for my transition to this stage. The selection of each has been carefully considered,
with differing hierarchical distance from one another, and differing backgrounds and training, which will be
complimentary for my fellowship.
My research support mentors will meet with me, as a group every 4-6 weeks to advise, mentor and support.
Depending on the stage of research being undertaken, each research mentor will take a lead role during my
fellowship, complimenting their specific expertise. Each research mentor has agreed that I can additionally contact
them on an ad-hoc basis for research or personal developmental advice, in their roles as international research
leaders.
Practice development
As a consultant pharmacist of >10 years, my practice in anticoagulation practice is already of a high standard.
Therefore, for practice development during my fellowship I will maintain my clinical practice currency, through
inpatient ward-rounds and outpatients anticoagulation clinics at KCH, whilst I undertake the research. I have
additionally and importantly invited Prof Taylor to be my practice development mentor. As a leading pharmacy
clinical academic,
sectdars__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_c.text:
Prof Taylor has translated his practice research to international clinical practice, which is what I
wish to emulate in the anticoagulation field. I will meet Prof Taylor every 2 months to practice mentor me and
advise on how to develop my international presence to translate my practice-research findings internationally.
Additionally, as my fellowship is focused on anticoagulation stewardship, I will undertake a practice visit with Dr
Alison Burnett, President of the Anticoagulation Forum (AF) in North America. AF are world leaders on
anticoagulation stewardship. I will learn about the initiatives that are in place and bring back learning to the UK. I
will also use this visit to foster a relationship with AF, to help with dissemination aspects of my fellowship.
Training
Implementation Science
A key reason for undertaking this fellowship is to formally learn about implementation science and its application
in the healthcare setting. I will take the following module at Oxford University: Knowledge into Action.
This particular module was chosen, based on the learning outcomes, the opportunity it provides to work on the
UFH problem as part of the assignment that is completed, the oppor
sectdars__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_d.text:
tunity it will provide to interact and network
with other healthcare professionals and students tackling implementation projects in their specific fields and the
timing of when the module runs. The residential component of the module runs in May of each year. Considering
when I plan to start the fellowship, May time of the first year of the fellowship is the perfect time to learn about
implementation science, as discussions on implementation will begin as I enter year 2 of the fellowship. This
module provides a sound foundation in this field.
The module is run over an eight-week period, using a hybrid teaching method, providing additionally flexibility.
This module incorporates a 1 week residential at Oxford for face-to-face teaching, which will afford me the
opportunity to network with others doing implementation work. The assessment for this module, is a 4000-word
assignment, which will be anchored on the UFH protocol implementation work. I will also attend the NIHR ARC
South London Implementation Science Conference and the International Quality and Safety in Healthcare
Conference in the final year of my fellowship, to further network and learn from experts working in the
impleme
sectdars__001_d.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_e.text:
ntation science field, as I implement and evaluate the developed UFH protocol.
Epidemiology
Study 2 has an epidemiology component, which I am not formally trained in. I will therefore undertake 3 distance
learning modules from the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine master’s programme in
epidemiology:
EPM101–Fundamentals in Epidemiology
EPM102–Statistics for Epidemiology
EPM103–Practical Epidemiology
The content of the modules is learnt through self-direction of materials provided and will allow me to complete the
material at appropriate times during year 1 of my fellowship, to support study 2, and provide the requisite
foundation required in this discipline.
Personal development
Leadership
During my fellowship, I will refine my leadership skills and complete selected e-learning modules, from the NIHR
leadership lab modules to address personal developmental objectives and my fellowship proposal:
-Collaborating across organisational boundaries
-Courageous and compelling conversations
-Building resilience to navigate complexity and change
-My leadership brand
-Building strength in my team
-Leading change and innovation
These modules will be undertaken at appropriate times
sectdars__001_e.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_f.text:
during my fellowship, so the learning can be applied in
real-time.
Coaching
As part of my personal effectiveness development, I will undertake a series of coaching sessions in the final year
of my fellowship, in the order to reflect and develop the right attributes which would ensure my personal
effectiveness on the international stage. The coaching will provide a non-directive form of development, through a
series of purposeful conversations which will enable me to move towards achieving my research, practice and
personal goals. The coaching is scheduled for the final year of my fellowship, as that is the time, I will be
addressing the implementation phases of the research and liaising with organisations in the UK and
internationally.
Research Support
RESEARCH SUPPORT 1
Name Joseph Standing
Institution UCL
Position Professor of Pharmacometrics
Description of support provided
Prof Standing holds a PhD in clinical pharmacology, and MSc in Statistics. He is a pharmacist by background and
a member of the Royal Pharmaceutical Society, British Pharmacological Society, Fellow of the Royal Statistical
Society, member of the European Society for Clinical Microbiology and Infectious Disease
sectdars__001_f.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_g.text:
s and member of the
International Society of Pharmacometrics. Prof Standing has held two MRC academic fellowships (Methodology
2011-2015, Clinician Scientist 2015-2020) which have led to the formation of his pharmacometrics research group
at the UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health. His group is funded from research council and European
agencies (MRC, EU-FP7, Swedish Research Council) and charities/non-profit (Wellcome, DNDi, GOSHCC). Prof
Standing is the MSc module lead for the Pharmacometrics module at UCL, where students gain theoretical and
practical computational knowledge on population PKPD. Prof Standing also teaching pharmacometrics
internationally on an ad-hoc basis - recently includes Kenya and Malaysia. Prof Standing has a vibrant research
programme, having supervised 9 PhD students to completion and currently supervises 9 PhD students. The
academic background of these students ranges from pharmacy, medicine, and biology to physics and engineering.
Prof Standing has medication regulatory and industrial collaboration experience, having served 3 years on the
European Medicines Agency (EMA) Modelling and Simulation Working Party, been on several EMA expert grou
sectdars__001_g.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_h.text:
ps
(mainly on paediatrics and infectious diseases), and has current active industry collaborations with AstraZeneca,
BenevolentAI and Lilly. Prof Standing is the perfect research support mentor for study 1 of my fellowship.
RESEARCH SUPPORT 2
Name Bryony Dean Franklin
Institution UCL & Imperial College Healthcare NHS Trust
Position Professor of Medication Safety
Description of support provided
Prof Franklin is a hospital pharmacist and Director of the Centre for Medication Safety and Service Quality, a joint
research unit between Imperial College Healthcare NHS Trust and UCL School of Pharmacy, where Prof Franklin
is Professor of Medication Safety. Prof Franklin is co-director of the NIHR West London Patient Safety Research
Collaboration and theme lead for the NIHR Health Protection Research Unit in Healthcare Associated Infection
and Antimicrobial Resistance at Imperial College London.
Prof Franklin has been involved with patient safety research for more than 25 years; she has published widely on
medication safety, the evaluation of various technologies designed to reduce errors, and the patient’s role in
patient safety. Her current work combines research, quality improvement, edu
sectdars__001_h.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_i.text:
cation and training, and hospital
pharmacy practice. Prof Franklin is Co-Editor-in-Chief for the journal BMJ Quality and Safety. She is also co-editor
of the textbook “Safety in Medication Use” and co-author of “Going into Hospital? A guide for patients, carers and
families”, a book aimed at the general public. Prof Franklin is currently and has in the past undertaken studies
using National Reporting and Learning System data. Coupled with her research programme in medication safety,
Prof Franklin is the ideal research support mentor for study 2 of my fellowship.
RESEARCH SUPPORT 3
Name Louise Rose
Institution King's College London
Position Professor of Critical Care Nursing
Description of support provided
Prof Rose is a nurse by background and professor of Critical Care Nursing at KCL and an honorary Professor in
Critical Care at the Lane Fox Respiratory Unit at St Thomas’ Hospital. Prof Rose has published over 205 peer-
reviewed publications and authored four book chapters. She has been awarded 106 peer reviewed competitive
research grants totalling >£10 million. Prior to joining KCL, Prof Rose was Associate Professor at the University of
Toronto and remains the Research Director
sectdars__001_i.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_j.text:
of the Prolonged Ventilation Weaning Centre there. Prof Rose’s
research program focuses on improving outcomes and the healthcare experience of mechanically ventilated
patients across the care continuum from the emergency department, intensive care unit, specialised weaning
centre, and at home. Through her collaborations with specialised weaning centres in both Canada and the UK,
Professor Rose has a particular research interest in the delivery of care to long-stay ICU patients and their
families, and ICU survivorship. Prof Rose has extensive clinical experience of intensive care in four countries and
has set up a Masters in Critical Care Nursing at Addis Ababa University in Ethiopia. During the COVID pandemic,
Prof Rose co-founded and implemented Life-Lines, a philanthropic initiative that provided >1,400 4G enabled
tablets with a bespoke ICU virtual visiting solution to >180 NHS hospitals. Prof Rose has extensive experience of
implementation, particularly in the application of the CFIR, having widely implemented initiatives in the critical care
setting internationally and will provide research support in the implementation aspects of my fellowship (study 3).
Practice Support
PRACT
sectdars__001_j.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_k.text:
ICE SUPPORT 1
Name David Taylor
Institution South London and the Maudsley Hospital
Director of Pharmacy and Pathology & Professor of
Position
Psychopharmacology
How will the proposed practice support arrangements contribute to the development of the applicant?
Prof Taylor, is Director of Pharmacy and Pathology at the Maudsley Hospital and Professor of
Psychopharmacology at King’s College London, as well as head of the Pharmaceutical Sciences Clinical
Academic Group at King’s Health Partners. He is the Editor-in-Chief of the journal Therapeutic Advances in
Psychopharmacology and the lead author of the Maudsley Prescribing Guidelines, which since their inception in
1993 has sold over 300,000 copies in fourteen editions and twelve languages. In addition Prof Taylor serves on
the editorial board of several journals. Prof Taylor has authored over 400 clinical papers with >21K citations and a
H-index of 68. As well being a Fellow of the Royal Pharmaceutical Society of Great Britain, Prof Taylor is a fellow
of the Royal College of Physicians (Edin) and an Honorary Fellow of the Royal College of Psychiatrists.
As a clinical academic, Prof Taylor has built an international reputation in the
sectdars__001_k.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_l.text:
field of psychiatric medicine based
on his clinical practice and research. I have chosen Prof Taylor to mentor me as a practice mentor during my
fellowship, as he is the perfect exemplar of an international clinical academic in his field. He maintains his clinical
practice with a vibrate research programme. Prof Taylor will guide and mentor me to develop as an international
clinical academic leader in the anticoagulation field.
Collaborations
The collaborations I have fostered and developed will ensure success and uptake of the UFH protocol developed.
Already established:
1. Guy’s and St. Thomas’ Hospital and the Royal Brompton and Harefield Hospitals
Prof Beverley Hunt, lead coagulation haematologist at GSTT and RBHH and the haematology teams based there
have agreed to collaborate on the proposal. This will mean the Population Pharmacokinetic Modelling work will be
validated in these 2 external centres using prospectively collected data, ensuring the credibility of the final model
developed. I will also be implementing the final UFH model developed at these external centres.
2. British Society for Haematology (BSH)
The BSH promotes excellence in the study, research, and practice
sectdars__001_l.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_m.text:
of haematology. The BSH is responsible for
writing UK haematology guidelines, including thrombosis and haemostasis guidelines. The last society guideline
on the use and monitoring of heparin44 was published by the BSH in 2006 and requires updating. The chair of the
BSH thrombosis and haemostasis task force, Dr Keith Gomez, has confirmed the importance of the work and I
have been invited to be a member of the UFH guideline writing group which will be updating this guidance in the
future. This will position me well, for using the work from this fellowship to update this guideline, ensuring
widespread uptake and dissemination.
3. Thrombosis UK
This UK charity increases awareness, supports research and extends understanding through education and the
sharing of information to improve care for all those affected by thrombosis. Jo Jerrome, chief executive of the
charity, is fully supportive of the work proposed. The charity has nominated a patient to be part of the PPI panel for
my fellowship work. Thrombosis UK will also help with widely disseminating the work through their communication
channels with patients, members of the public, carers and healthcare professionals.
4. The United Kin
sectdars__001_m.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_n.text:
gdom Clinical Pharmacy Association (UKCPA)
An organisation of UK clinical pharmacists, promotes expert practice in medicines management. The organisation
provides a perfect platform to reach front-line pharmacists across the UK to share the development of the UFH
protocol and promote best practice. The Chair of the Haemostasis and Thrombosis sub-group of the UKCPA has
also endorsed the work proposed in the application and will support with developmental and dissemination aspects
of the work undertaken.
5. Anticoagulation Forum
An international organisation helping practitioners improve anticoagulation patient care. Its membership includes
more than 13,000 healthcare professionals representing over 3,000 anticoagulation services. Anticoagulation
Forum has exceptional influence on the practice of anticoagulation. I have already connected with the
organisation’s president, Dr Allison Burnett, who has agreed to host me and to share best practice in the UFH field
and the anticoagulation stewardship work they lead on, in USA. I will use this opportunity to ensure that the work
from my fellowship feeds into the work of Anticoagulation Forum and foster this relationship to ensure worldwide
sectdars__001_n.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_o.text:
dissemination of the resultant UFH work.
Post fellowship:
International Society of Thrombosis and Haemostasis (ISTH)
Is the leading organisation dedicated to the prevention and treatment of thrombosis. The ISTH brings together
clinicians from >100 countries, with ISTH guidelines having world-wide impact. The Scientific and Standardisation
Committee (SSC) on control of anticoagulation of ISTH, is focused on laboratory and clinical issues related to the
use of anticoagulant drugs. During the latter part of my fellowship, I will apply to be a committee member of the
control of anticoagulation SSC, which will afford me the opportunity to champion the new protocol in the
organisation with the aim of the heparin work informing a ISTH specific guideline from the SSC.
Host Organisation Support Statement
The following statement of support must be completed collaboratively by the heads of department at the Host
Organisation (who will administer any award) and the Partner Organisation.
The Heads of department will be able to access this section of the form once they have confirmed participation.
Invitations to participate are sent by the applicant via the 'Participants and Signatories' secti
sectdars__001_o.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_p.text:
on of the form.
Statement of support
The department of haematological medicine at King’s College Hospital NHS Foundation Trust (KCHFT) and the
Institute of Pharmaceutical Sciences at King’s College London (KCL) welcomes the opportunity to host Jignesh’s
NIHR advanced clinical academic fellowship.
KCHFT is one of the UK’s largest and busiest teaching Trusts with King’s specialist services available to patients
across a wide catchment area, providing nationally and internationally recognised work in liver disease and
transplantation, neurosciences, haemato-oncology and fetal medicine. KCHFT houses two hyper-acute stroke
units, the largest bone marrow transplant centre in Europe, and is one of London’s four trauma centres. In
partnership with King’s College London (KCL), the Trust has recently been awarded a National Research Centre in
Patient Safety and Service Quality.
The haematology department, where Jignesh will be based, is one of the UK’s largest, with an extensive referral
base. There is a vibrant research culture within the department which has demonstrated continued success in
leading recruitment to many national and international clinical trials. Our departments research pu
sectdars__001_p.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_q.text:
blishes in high
impact peer reviewed journals, e.g. N Engl J Med (2022) 386:11-23; Blood (2022) 139:2642-2652; Blood (2020)
136:1347–1350, demonstrating the extensive and innovative research taking place. We make significant
contributions to the ever-evolving body of knowledge and helping advance care and treatment options for our
patients. At any one time, we have a number of research students (PhD, MD, MSc students (including
international)) and post-doctoral students based in the department. In addition to this, our department currently
hosts 3 NIHR/MRC CARP awards, held by 3 consultant haematologists and Jignesh will directly benefit from this
exposure during his fellowship. The thrombosis team at King’s is a leading multidisciplinary clinical academic
group within the department. During the last 10 years, 6 doctors have completed their MD(Res) studies and 4
pharmacists have successfully completed their PhD’s within this group, with 4 specifically conducting
pharmacokinetic studies as part of their research programmes. During his fellowship, Jignesh will attend the
monthly research group meeting, to report on progress and to provide a steer on other studies ongoing within the
g
sectdars__001_q.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_r.text:
roup.
There is a structured partnership in place between KCHFT and KCL through the Academic Health Science Centre
of King’s Health Partners. As a clinical academic, Jignesh will benefit from the links at the Institute of
Pharmaceutical Science in KCL, where research activity revolves around three strands: i) drug discovery, ii)
medicines development and iii) medicines use. In the REF 2021 exercise, KCL’s submission for Allied Health
Professions, Dentistry, Nursing and Pharmacy (UoA3) was ranked 1st of 90 submissions overall in the UK (by both
power and GPA) and 1st for Environment (GPA 4.00), Impacts (GPA 3.86) and for the number of 4* Outputs.
Indeed, Jignesh’s research featured, as part of the environment statement which was returned under this UoA3.
The Institute of Pharmaceutical Science (IPS) has a strong track record of supporting clinical academics to realise
their research ambitions, with >15 clinical pharmacists completing their PhDs within King’s Health Partners during
the past 10 years. IPS staff supervise over 100 PhD students and monthly PhD student-led research seminars
facilitate networking and discussion across our postgraduate research community culminating in the
sectdars__001_r.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_s.text:
IPS
Postgraduate Research Symposium which is a highlight of the year.
Both our departments have clear commitments to championing initiatives and processes that facilitate the
progression of staff from underrepresented groups into leadership positions and ensuring that EDI initiatives and
principles translate into meaningful and quantifiable positive changes at all levels. Our departments also fully
committed to supporting the wellbeing and equality of opportunity for our student / staff community and the delivery
of zero tolerance to bullying, harassment and discrimination.
Several initiatives span King’s Health Partners which Jignesh will continue to benefit from during the fellowship,
e.g. the King’s Clinical Academic Training Office (KCATO), providing trainee-centred guidance to navigate the
career journey so that King’s clinical academics emerge as highly skilled clinicians with the ability to deliver
globally impactful research, as well as being inspirational role models. In addition, the Advanced Practice
Education & Research Hub, hosted by the Florence Nightingale School of Nursing & Midwifery, will expose
Jignesh to a wider network of allied health professionals who are als
sectdars__001_s.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_t.text:
o developing and succeeding in clinical
academic careers. Finally, Jignesh will benefit from the world leading seminars that are held across King’s Health
Partners, where researchers from around the world are invited to present and share their research.
Support for Jignesh
Jignesh is a high-achieving clinical academic in our departments, and widely acknowledged to be a rising star in
the field of thrombosis and anticoagulation. He has a strong academic portfolio, with several important publications
in internationally recognised journals to his name. He is a highly organised, collegiate and committed researcher
who has established research collaborations across King’s and with other groups around the UK and further afield.
His research has always been highly patient-centric, dealing with important and unanswered issues in
anticoagulation. He was hosted in our departments through his very successful GSTT/KCL BRC PhD fellowship
(2009-2012), where he modelled enoxaparin pharmacokinetics during the antenatal period. Hosting Jignesh
through this fellowship, where he plans to model unfractionated heparin pharmacokinetics aligns with the
departments expertise and capability and the wider K
sectdars__001_t.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_u.text:
ing’s Health Partners mission to create and nurture the next
generation of the NHS clinical academic workforce. The fellowship is also an appropriate next step for Jignesh, in
becoming an international research leader in the field of anticoagulation practice and a role model for academic
healthcare professionals, particularly pharmacy clinical academics. As an existing member of our departments,
Jignesh has made full use of the research support infrastructure in preparation for his application and will continue
to receive such support. In the long-term, we have an infrastructure ready to ensure Jignesh achieves his research
ambitions which will benefit patients. In our view he would be an ideal recipient for this award.
sectdars__001_u.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sub_criteria.name: Common Characteristics of Good Applications
sub_criteria.definition: The application demonstrates characteristics commonly found in strong grant applications.

signals.signal_text: Clear research vision and career trajectory
Distinctive ideas with an independent direction
Well-justified training plan
Strong mentorship and institutional support

sub_criteria.pros: The application demonstrates a clear research vision focused on anticoagulation safety and a distinct career trajectory towards international leadership (g.1.a, g.1.b). The training plan is exceptionally well-justified, explicitly linking courses in implementation science and epidemiology to specific project needs and career gaps (g.1.c). Strong mentorship is evidenced by a team of senior researchers with extensive track records in relevant fields (g.1.d).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the vision is clear, the specific 'distinctive ideas' regarding the novel aspects of the implementation strategy could be slightly more elaborated to fully distinguish it from standard implementation studies, though the co-development aspect is strong.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the vision is clear, the specific 'distinctive ideas' regarding the novel aspects of the implementation strategy could be slightly more elaborated to fully distinguish it from standard implementation studies, though the co-development aspect is strong.

evidence.text:
Dental
Journal.
Reason for fellowship application
I am a national leader relating to research and practice in the safe and effective use of anticoagulants. This
fellowship and the work outlined in my fellowship application will afford me the time and space to tackle the
problem of dosing, monitoring, and safety of unfractionated heparin, the results of which will directly translate to
clinical practice in the NHS. The fellowship will also provide me with specific training and experience in the field of
implementation science; an area which I have little knowledge and skill in, but which holds huge potential in
ensuring the translation of the work I undertake both through this fellowship and in the future.
Impact on career
I aim to be an international research leader in the field of anticoagulation therapy research and practice. This
fellowship and the training received will provide me with the required platform to achieve this and develop and
refine my personal skills and attributes required to function effectively at this level.
Title of research degree PhD in Pharmacokinetics
Has the degree already
Yes
been awarded?
Date of Award 01/03/2013
Has this application, or a similar app

lication, previously been submitted to this or any other funding body?
Yes
If Yes, provide details
I applied through an NIHR Advanced Fellowship (round 9) for this project - ref: NIHR303192. Key feedback
received on not progressing the application by the reviewers was focussed on the single centre nature of the
original application - whereby the original application was developing the pharmacokinetic model and
implementing the UFH protocol developed in a single centre study, at King's College Hospital only.
The reviewers also highlighted the implementation aspects of the work required more details.
Taking this feedback on-board, the proposed research is now multi-centre, with Guy's and St. Thomas' Hospital
and the Royal Brompton and Harefield Hospitals. This means that in total 6 hospital sites which will be involved in
my fellowship work (KCH - Denmark Hill, KCH - Princess Royal Hospital, Guy's Hospital, St.Thomas' Hospital,
Royal Brompton Hospital and Harefield Hospital). These partners will enhance the work proposed and ensure the
credibility of the final heparin dosing and monitoring protocol developed. All these hospital sites will be
implementing the UFH protocol.
Details on

Proposed training and development programme
Gap analysis
In order to establish myself as an international research leader, a gap analysis was conducted. Using the vitae
researcher development framework, the following were identified under the domain of personal effectiveness:
i. Has national, international and policy-making network connections with academic and non-academic
bodies and organisations.
ii. Has influential connections with significant bodies and organisations.
In addition, I have identified that I have little knowledge of the field of implementation science and the field of
epidemiology, which I will formally study during my fellowship.
Through completing the work proposed in my fellowship, the step-change in my career will occur, which will
establish me as an international leader and authority on anticoagulation therapy and with that, have exceptional
influence, with input sought from policy makers, as befits an internationally recognised researcher.
Research support
Suitable research support mentors for each stage of the research proposed in the application have been
identified, who would not only enhance the work undertaken but are international research leaders in
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
ntation science field, as I implement and evaluate the developed UFH protocol.
Epidemiology
Study 2 has an epidemiology component, which I am not formally trained in. I will therefore undertake 3 distance
learning modules from the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine master’s programme in
epidemiology:
EPM101–Fundamentals in Epidemiology
EPM102–Statistics for Epidemiology
EPM103–Practical Epidemiology
The content of the modules is learnt through self-direction of materials provided and will allow me to complete the
material at appropriate times during year 1 of my fellowship, to support study 2, and provide the requisite
foundation required in this discipline.
Personal development
Leadership
During my fellowship, I will refine my leadership skills and complete selected e-learning modules, from the NIHR
leadership lab modules to address personal developmental objectives and my fellowship proposal:
-Collaborating across organisational boundaries
-Courageous and compelling conversations
-Building resilience to navigate complexity and change
-My leadership brand
-Building strength in my team
-Leading change and innovation
These modules will be undertaken at appropriate times
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
during my fellowship, so the learning can be applied in
real-time.
Coaching
As part of my personal effectiveness development, I will undertake a series of coaching sessions in the final year
of my fellowship, in the order to reflect and develop the right attributes which would ensure my personal
effectiveness on the international stage. The coaching will provide a non-directive form of development, through a
series of purposeful conversations which will enable me to move towards achieving my research, practice and
personal goals. The coaching is scheduled for the final year of my fellowship, as that is the time, I will be
addressing the implementation phases of the research and liaising with organisations in the UK and
internationally.
Research Support
RESEARCH SUPPORT 1
Name Joseph Standing
Institution UCL
Position Professor of Pharmacometrics
Description of support provided
Prof Standing holds a PhD in clinical pharmacology, and MSc in Statistics. He is a pharmacist by background and
a member of the Royal Pharmaceutical Society, British Pharmacological Society, Fellow of the Royal Statistical
Society, member of the European Society for Clinical Microbiology and Infectious Disease
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sub_criteria.name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award
sub_criteria.definition: The applicant clearly articulates why this specific award is the right next step in their career.
sub_criteria.group_name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award

signals.signal_text: Explain why this award is the right next step in your career (not just another project grant)
What difference would securing an award make to your career, and why now?
How will an award build on your existing experience and what new skills will it enable you to gain?
How will it develop your research partnerships within the NHS, public health or social care, charity organisations, industry or other organisations?

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant clearly articulates why this award is the right next step, specifically to gain implementation science skills and transition from single-centre to multi-centre work, directly addressing prior reviewer feedback (g.2.a, g.2.b). The award will build on existing PK expertise by adding critical implementation and epidemiology skills (g.2.c). Partnerships with NHS trusts and international forums (New Mexico) are explicitly planned to expand research networks (g.2.d).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The description of how the award will develop partnerships with industry or charity organizations is less detailed compared to the NHS and academic partnerships, though Thrombosis UK is mentioned.
sub_criteria.rationale: The description of how the award will develop partnerships with industry or charity organizations is less detailed compared to the NHS and academic partnerships, though Thrombosis UK is mentioned.

sub_criteria.name: Relevant Research Experience and Training
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrates prior research experience and/or training that prepares the applicant to successfully undertake the proposed award in applied health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of previous projects, research methods, or techniques relevant to the proposed study
Evidence of formal training, courses, or certifications
Alignment between prior experience and skills required for the proposed project

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant has extensive prior experience in population pharmacokinetic modelling (PPM), R, NONMEM, and SPSS, directly relevant to Study 1 (g.3.a). Formal training is evidenced by a PhD in Pharmacokinetics and previous grant leadership (g.3.b). There is a perfect alignment between the applicant's proven skills in PK modelling and the technical requirements of the proposed project (g.3.c).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the applicant's background is highly aligned with the project's technical demands.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the applicant's background is highly aligned with the project's technical demands.
